mappings.rationaleAI: Covers the nature and scope of literature (A.1), diverse literary subfields and genre systems (A.6), academic study including literary theory, criticism, and textual scholarship (A.7), and the full range of literary forms from fiction to non-fiction and children's literature (C).

Directly covers metre, rhyme, alliteration, and assonance (B.2.a.i–ii), prosody across verse cultures (B.2.b), epic and lyric genres (C.2.a, C.4), and the distinction between verse and prose (C.1), filling all priority prosody paths.

Addresses the nature of literary fiction versus non-fiction (A.1), genre fiction versus literary fiction as classification systems (A.6), and directly covers the novel, novella, and short story (C.2.d–e).

War and Peace and Anna Karenina are canonical examples of the realist novel (C.2.d) within the Western literary tradition (D.1); his late philosophical writings also represent nonfictional prose literature (C.6.e–f).
The Aeneid is the paradigmatic literary epic (C.2.a), the Eclogues exemplify pastoral lyric (C.4.b), and all three works are foundational to Western literary history (D.1).

Covers literary realism and theatrical realism as named genre-defining movements (A.6), tracing realism's extension from painting through literature, theatre, and cinema within the Western tradition (D.1).
Directly and exclusively covers the short story form and its antecedents (C.2.e), from ancient folk tales to the modern literary short story crystallized in the 19th century.

Theatre is the medium for dramatic and theatrical literature (C.3), encompassing tragedy, comedy, and musical forms; its connection to literature (A.5) as a performing arts integration point is explicit in the article.
The Masnavi is a six-volume Persian epic poem (C.2.a) and the Divan-e Shams is lyric poetry (C.4); both exemplify non-Western literary traditions outside the D.1 Western peoples path.

The Divine Comedy combines epic narrative (C.2.a) with religious drama (C.3.f); De Vulgari Eloquentia is a theoretical treatise on literary language (A.7), and Dante is foundational to Western literary history (D.1).

The Iliad and Odyssey are the defining examples of oral-formulaic epic (C.2.a) and Homeric prosodic style (B.2.b); the Homeric Question represents scholarly-critical writing on literature (A.7), foundational to Western literary history (D.1).
The Tale of Genji is widely considered the world's first novel (C.2.d) and her waka poetry exemplifies lyric tradition (C.4); both are foundational to non-Western literary history outside D.1.

Romanticism as a literary genre-defining movement encompasses Gothic romance and Romantic poetry (C.2.c, A.6), and its trajectory from 1800–1850 is a defining episode in Western literary history (D.1).

Directly defines the distinction between fiction and non-fiction as literary categories (A.1) and covers the full range of nonfictional prose forms — biography, journalism, essay, history, creative non-fiction — in path C.6.

Plato's dialogues are the canonical examples of philosophical dialogue as literary form (C.6.k) and philosophical literature (C.6.e); his works are foundational texts in the theoretical study of literature (A.7 via Poetics connections).

Shakespeare's 39 plays span tragedy (C.3.a), comedy (C.3.b), and romance forms; his 154 sonnets are canonical language-based lyrics (C.4.b); and his works are central to drama theory and Western literary scholarship (A.7, D.1).

Directly maps to path D (history of literature), tracing written and oral narrative from ancient Mesopotamia through all major world literary traditions; explicitly addresses genre evolution and printing history.

The Poetics is the foundational theoretical treatise on tragedy and dramatic composition (A.7, C.3.a), and the Rhetoric covers elements of discourse and persuasion (B.1) foundational to the literary analysis of language.

Approximately 1,000 surviving lyric poems celebrating nature, friendship, and the moon exemplify lyric literary production (C.4) within the Chinese literary tradition (D), outside the Western peoples path.

Candide exemplifies satirical literature (C.5); his histories are history as literature (C.6.b); his philosophical tales and letters represent philosophical and political literature (C.6.e–f); all within the Western literary tradition (D.1).

Directly and comprehensively covers path C.2.d (the novel and the tale), tracing its history from Greco-Roman prose fiction through Cervantes, 18th-century British novelists, parallel Chinese and Japanese traditions, and contemporary digital forms; also addresses genre classification (A.6).

Covers beat, pulse, metre, and tempo as rhythmic elements of prosody (B.2.a.i) and their application across music, poetry, and speech, filling priority paths B.2 and B.2.a.i which have no VSI recommendations.
A Vindication of the Rights of Woman and Vindication of the Rights of Men are canonical examples of political and polemical literature (C.6.f–g) by a foundational Enlightenment-era writer.

Mesopotamia is the origin of cuneiform writing and the world's earliest literary texts (Epic of Gilgamesh implied by 'literary traditions'), placing it at the historical starting point of path D (history of literature).

Opera is a theatrical form built on sung dramatic texts (librettos), directly relevant to dramatic literature (C.3) and the integration of literature with other arts including music and staging (A.5).

The Bible encompasses law, narrative, poetry (Psalms as hymnody, C.4.a), prophecy, and epistle as literary genres (C.6.d), and its status as both religious document and literary foundation raises fundamental questions about the nature of literature (A.1).

The Bhagavad Gita is framed as a philosophical dialogue (C.6.k) embedded within a larger epic narrative, and its 18 chapters constitute canonical doctrinal and religious literature (C.6.d) with classical commentaries constituting literary-critical writing.

Oral tradition directly covers primitive, folk, and popular literature (C.8) including epic poetry (C.2.a) and ballads (C.2.g), and its analysis — from Homeric epic to African griot practice — is foundational to understanding pre-literate literary history (D).

Folk music traditions include folk ballads and oral song forms (C.2.g), and the article's grounding in orally transmitted, community-based cultural expression connects it to primitive and folk literature (C.8).

The Book of Healing is an encyclopedic philosophical and scientific work constituting philosophical literature (C.6.e) and scientific literature (C.6.h); Avicenna also wrote poetry, placing him within the non-Western literary tradition.
